quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Z-score"?

options.text: A standardized value calculated as z = (x - μ)/σ indicating how many standard deviations an observation lies from the mean
A study in which the researcher observes and records data without imposing a treatment or intervention on the subjects
Tests H₀: μ₁ = μ₂ (or μ₁ - μ₂ = 0) using t = (x̄₁ - x̄₂)/√(s₁²/n₁ + s₂²/n₂)
A smooth curve that models the overall shape of a distribution; the total area under the curve equals 1 and represents probability

quiz_questions.explanation: "Z-score" is defined as: A standardized value calculated as z = (x - μ)/σ indicating how many standard deviations an observation lies from the mean. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Percentile"?

options.text: The proportion of variation in y explained by the linear relationship with x; ranges from 0 to 1
One-sided: Hₐ: p > p₀ or p < p₀ (p-value is area in one tail); Two-sided: Hₐ: p ≠ p₀ (p-value is area in both tails)
The percentage of observations in a distribution that fall at or below a given value
SD(X ± Y) = √(Var(X) + Var(Y)) for independent random variables; note that SDs do NOT simply add

quiz_questions.explanation: "Percentile" is defined as: The percentage of observations in a distribution that fall at or below a given value. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Standard normal table (z-table)"?

options.text: A ∪ B ("A or B") includes outcomes in either event; A ∩ B ("A and B") includes only outcomes in both events
As the number of trials increases, the sample proportion of successes converges toward the true probability
A table giving cumulative probabilities (areas to the left) for z-scores in the standard normal distribution
One-sample t-test applied to the differences: t = d̄/(sd/√n) with df = n-1, testing H₀: μd = 0

quiz_questions.explanation: "Standard normal table (z-table)" is defined as: A table giving cumulative probabilities (areas to the left) for z-scores in the standard normal distribution. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Standardization"?

options.text: V = √(χ²/(n·min(r-1, c-1))); ranges from 0 to 1, measuring the strength of association between two categorical variables
The process of converting raw scores to z-scores, making different distributions comparable on a common scale
Tests H₀: μ₁ = μ₂ (or μ₁ - μ₂ = 0) using t = (x̄₁ - x̄₂)/√(s₁²/n₁ + s₂²/n₂)
The long-run proportion of intervals that capture the true parameter; e.g., 95% means 95 out of 100 intervals would contain p

quiz_questions.explanation: "Standardization" is defined as: The process of converting raw scores to z-scores, making different distributions comparable on a common scale. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Relative standing"?

options.text: Randomly selecting entire groups (clusters) and surveying all individuals within chosen clusters; practical when a population list is unavailable
(O - E)²/E for each cell; identifies which observed-expected discrepancies drive the overall test result
An observation's position within a distribution, typically expressed as a z-score or percentile rank
Using df = min(n₁-1, n₂-1) when technology is unavailable; produces wider intervals and larger p-values

quiz_questions.explanation: "Relative standing" is defined as: An observation's position within a distribution, typically expressed as a z-score or percentile rank. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A standardized value calculated as z = (x - μ)/σ indicating how many standard deviations an observation lies from the mean"?

options.text: Mutually exclusive events
Z-score
Within-block variability
Coefficient of determination (r²)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Z-score".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The percentage of observations in a distribution that fall at or below a given value"?

options.text: Percentile
Replication
Multiplication rule for independents
t-distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Percentile".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A table giving cumulative probabilities (areas to the left) for z-scores in the standard normal distribution"?

options.text: Heavy tails
Standard normal table (z-table)
Significance test for μ
Homogeneity vs. independence

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Standard normal table (z-table)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The process of converting raw scores to z-scores, making different distributions comparable on a common scale"?

options.text: CLT for proportions
Population regression model
Null hypothesis for slope
Standardization

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Standardization".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An observation's position within a distribution, typically expressed as a z-score or percentile rank"?

options.text: Zero in the interval
Relative standing
Mean difference (d̄)
Statistical significance

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Relative standing".

options.text: Tests H₀: μ₁ = μ₂ (or μ₁ - μ₂ = 0) using t = (x̄₁ - x̄₂)/√(s₁²/n₁ + s₂²/n₂)
V = √(χ²/(n·min(r-1, c-1))); ranges from 0 to 1, measuring the strength of association between two categorical variables

Randomly selecting entire groups (clusters) and surveying all individuals within chosen clusters; practical when a population list is unavailable
An observation's position within a distribution, typically expressed as a z-score or percentile rank
Using df = min(n₁-1, n₂-1) when technology is unavailable; produces wider intervals and larger p-values

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Standardization and Z-Scores is accurate?

options.text: Confidence Intervals for Proportions estimate an unknown population proportion p using the sample proportion p̂ ± z*√(p̂(1-p̂)/n), where z* is the critical value for the desired confidence level.
Standardization and Z-Scores transform raw data values into a common scale by expressing each observation as the number of standard deviations it falls above or below the mean: z = (x - μ)/σ.
Two-way tables (contingency tables) organize counts for two categorical variables and allow calculation of marginal, joint, and conditional distributions.
Experiments use random assignment to eliminate confounding, allowing causal conclusions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Standardization and Z-Scores as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Standardization and Z-Scores?

options.text: The width of the interval is determined by the confidence level (higher → wider), sample size (larger → narrower), and sample variability (larger s → wider).
Z-scores allow meaningful comparison of values from different distributions with different units, centers, and spreads.
Observational data can reveal associations but not causation, because confounding variables may drive the apparent relationship.
Significance Tests for Proportions test whether a population proportion p equals a hypothesized value p₀ using the test statistic z = (p̂ - p₀)/√(p₀(1-p₀)/n).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Standardization and Z-Scores as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Standardization and Z-Scores?

options.text: Looking up z-scores in the standard normal table (or using technology) converts them to percentiles and probabilities, enabling precise statements about relative standing.
Conditional Probability measures the probability of an event given that another event has occurred: P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B).
This distribution forms the basis for inference about population proportions using z-procedures.
For independent random variables: Var(X ± Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y)—variances always add, even for differences.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Standardization and Z-Scores as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Standardization and Z-Scores?

options.text: Interpreting Chi-Square Results requires understanding what a significant χ² value means and what it does not.
Standardization and Z-Scores transform raw data values into a common scale by expressing each observation as the number of standard deviations it falls above or below the mean: z = (x - μ)/σ.
The probability distribution table lists all possible values and their probabilities (which must sum to 1), enabling calculation of the mean μ = E(X) = Σ[x·P(x)] and variance σ² = Σ[(x-μ)²·P(x)].
Assessing normality through histograms, normal probability plots, and the Empirical Rule is essential before applying many inferential procedures.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Standardization and Z-Scores as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Standardization and Z-Scores?

options.text: Measures of Variability quantify the spread or dispersion of data, describing how much individual observations deviate from the center.
Z-scores allow meaningful comparison of values from different distributions with different units, centers, and spreads.
The interval provides a range of plausible values for the rate of change in y per unit change in x.
The slope b = r(sy/sx) represents the predicted change in y for a one-unit increase in x, while the y-intercept a gives the predicted y when x = 0.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Standardization and Z-Scores as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Standardization and Z-Scores?

options.text: Public health research testing whether smoking status (smoker/non-smoker) is independent of lung disease status (yes/no) in a random sample of adults
Educational researchers using a one-sample t-test to determine whether average ACT scores in a district differ from the national mean of 21
Quality control using CLT to construct control charts for process means even when individual measurements follow a skewed distribution
Comparing student performance across different exams by converting raw scores to z-scores (e.g., scoring 1.5σ above the mean on both math and English)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Standardization and Z-Scores.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Standardization and Z-Scores?

options.text: Medical diagnostics using z-scores to classify lab results as normal or abnormal based on population reference ranges
Modeling customer arrival times at a store using appropriate probability distributions to optimize staffing schedules
Market research testing whether product preference is associated with age group to determine whether different demographics need different marketing strategies
Student performance analysis in a classroom of 25 students using t-distribution (df = 24) rather than z-distribution because the true population standard deviation is unknown

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Standardization and Z-Scores.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Standardization and Z-Scores applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: College admissions comparing applicants who took different standardized tests (SAT vs. ACT) by converting to common z-score scale
Polling organizations reporting margin of error, which depends on the standard error σ/√n, explaining why larger polls are more precise
Portfolio diversification: the variance of a portfolio return equals the weighted sum of individual stock variances (plus covariance terms for non-independent stocks)
Market research using two-way tables to determine whether product preference varies by age group for targeted advertising strategies

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Standardization and Z-Scores.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Standardization and Z-Scores?

options.text: Demonstrating the CLT with simulations: rolling dice (uniform distribution) and plotting the mean of 30 rolls—the distribution of means is approximately normal despite the uniform population
Comparing student performance across different exams by converting raw scores to z-scores (e.g., scoring 1.5σ above the mean on both math and English)
Healthcare studies using cluster sampling of hospitals rather than individual patients when a complete patient list is impractical
Medical researchers estimating the rate at which blood pressure changes per year of age, with a confidence interval quantifying the uncertainty in the estimate

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Standardization and Z-Scores.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Standardization and Z-Scores in practice?

options.text: Epidemiological prospective cohort studies (like the Framingham Heart Study) tracking thousands of people over decades to identify cardiovascular risk factors
Economists checking LINE conditions before publishing a regression model of how education level predicts income
Medical diagnostics using z-scores to classify lab results as normal or abnormal based on population reference ranges
Pharmaceutical clinical trials using double-blind, placebo-controlled, randomized designs to test new drug efficacy while meeting FDA requirements

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Standardization and Z-Scores.
